model_findings.description: Three of four backends pass the user-supplied prompt as an argv element rather than over stdin: `_gemini_backend` uses `f"--prompt={prompt}"`, `_claude_backend` appends `prompt` as a positional arg, and `_hermes_backend` uses `f"--oneshot={prompt}"`. Only `_codex_backend` writes through stdin. This has two consequences. (1) Information leak: any other local user / process can read the full prompt via `ps`, `/proc/<pid>/cmdline`, or accounting logs — including credentials, PII, or proprietary content that callers reasonably assumed stayed within the chat envelope. (2) DoS / hard-failure surface: argv is bounded by `ARG_MAX` (~256 KB on macOS, ~128 KB-2 MB on Linux). A moderately sized chat history will silently `E2BIG` the exec and the client gets an opaque 502. Pipe prompts through stdin for all backends, as already done for codex.

`ChatCompletionRequest` has `extra="allow"` and `messages: list[Message]` with no upper bound, and the FastAPI app applies no body-size middleware. A single POST can carry hundreds of MB which the proxy will dutifully load, flatten into one giant string, and either (a) try to push through ARG_MAX-limited argv (see prompt-on-command-line finding) or (b) blow process memory before subprocess.run even runs. Add a `Content-Length` cap (e.g. 1 MiB) at the FastAPI layer plus a max `len(messages)` and per-message size validator on the Pydantic model.

`ChatCompletionRequest` declares `model_config = ConfigDict(extra="allow")`, so a client sending `stream: true` (the default for many OpenAI SDKs and most chat UIs) gets silently treated as non-streaming. The proxy returns a single `application/json` blob instead of the `text/event-stream` the client opened a streaming reader on, which surfaces as a hung connection or a parse error rather than a useful failure. Phase 1 docs say streaming is intentionally absent — fine — but the proxy must then reject `stream=true` with HTTP 400 and an OpenAI-shaped error (`type="invalid_request_error", code="streaming_not_supported"`) rather than pretend to honor it.

The proxy has zero auth: no `Authorization: Bearer ...` check, no shared-secret, no UDS option. That is defensible while bound to `127.0.0.1`, but the `--host` arg accepts arbitrary values without an explicit opt-in flag (open issue #2030), so a typo (`--host 0.0.0.0`) hands every model CLI on the developer's machine to anything on the LAN — at which point the lack of auth becomes RCE-adjacent (each backend can call shells, edit files, etc., depending on `--approval-mode`). At minimum, refuse to bind to non-loopback addresses unless an explicit `--allow-remote` flag is set, and require a token when remote binding is enabled.

On `CalledProcessError`, `TimeoutExpired`, and `OSError`, the proxy returns `_first_error_line(exc.stderr)` directly in the error message body. Agent CLIs routinely emit stack traces containing absolute repo paths, env-var-derived diagnostics, model API endpoint URLs, and occasionally bearer tokens or path-of-config-file hints in error frames. A remote (or browser-tab-via-CORS-misconfig) caller can fuzz request shapes to harvest filesystem and account topology. Strip stderr to a static taxonomy of error codes for the client; log the raw stderr only to server-side logs.

`_codex_backend` accepts `model` but ignores it and uses `os.environ.get("BRIDGE_PROXY_CODEX_MODEL", "gpt-5.5")` instead. The `/v1/models` endpoint advertises `"codex"` as a single id, so a caller who picks `"codex"` has no way to influence which underlying model runs — and a server-side env-var change silently re-routes their traffic. Either (a) drop the env var and pick a fixed model, or (b) expose distinct public model ids per codex variant in `_ROUTABLE_MODELS` and pass them through to `-m`.

`_message_content_to_text` keeps only items whose `type` is in `{None, "text", "input_text", "output_text"}`. The OpenAI envelope also carries `image_url`, `input_audio`, `file`, and tool-result parts. Those are dropped silently, which produces wrong-but-plausible completions (the model answers a vision question on text-only context). Phase 1 documents non-support of tool envelopes; extend that statement and either (a) reject requests containing unsupported content parts with HTTP 400, or (b) flag them in a per-request log. Silent data loss is the worst of the three options.

`/healthz` invokes `_probe_*` for every backend on every hit, each of which runs `<cli> --version` with a 1-second timeout. That is 4 forks + execs per request, up to 4 s of wall time, and routes that have hung CLIs (the issue #2036 hermes/anthropic logout scenario) penalize every health probe. Cache probe results for ~10 s, or split the heavy probe into `/healthz/deep` and have `/healthz` return only the proxy's own status.

Codex runs with `-s read-only`, Gemini with `--approval-mode plan`, Claude with `--bare` (no sandbox flag), and Hermes with no constraint at all. The proxy is documented as a chat-completions surface — none of these backends should be writing files or executing shells in response to proxy traffic. Pass an explicit non-mutating flag to each backend (claude `--permission-mode read` or equivalent; hermes whatever the analog is) so an attacker who lands a prompt-injection cannot escalate to disk writes via the chattier backends.

`extra="allow"` on `ChatCompletionRequest` causes `temperature`, `max_tokens`, `top_p`, `tools`, `tool_choice`, `response_format`, etc., to be accepted and ignored. Clients tuning a request will see their parameters have zero effect with no warning. Either thread the common ones through to backend CLIs where they map cleanly, or switch to `extra="forbid"` plus a documented allowlist so callers learn early that the proxy is narrower than OpenAI.

model_findings.description: The diagnostic block printed back to the writer on failure shows `error: "вмиваюся"` and `correction: "вмиваюся"` — identical values for the wrong form and the corrected form. The whole point of an error-correction item is that the two differ; a writer that copies this canonical example into its self-correction will produce a semantically broken item that still passes the schema gate. Use distinct forms (e.g. `error: "вмиваюшся"`, `correction: "вмиваюся"`).

When `items` is not a list (e.g. a writer emits `items: {...}` as a mapping, or `items: null`, or omits it entirely so the `.get("items", [])` default yields `[]`), the gate silently `continue`s with zero checked items and returns `passed=True`. An error-correction activity with no parseable items or with a structurally wrong items container should fail the gate, not pass it — otherwise a writer can suppress every alias violation by malforming the container.

Activity-type matching is exact-string lowercase: only `type: "error-correction"` triggers the schema. Plausible writer mistakes like `errorCorrection`, `error_correction`, `Error-Correction`, or `errorcorrection` cause `allowed_fields is None` and the gate skips the activity entirely. The gate's stated purpose is to catch writer mistakes that look plausible but bypass the schema contract — type-name aliases fall squarely in that class and are not covered. Either normalize `activity_type` (lowercase + hyphenate) or add a sibling check that flags unknown-but-similar types for `error-correction`.

The forbidden-alias map covers single-word variants (`wrong`, `incorrect`, `mistake`, `bad`, `correct`, `right`, `fix`, `fixed`) but is missing the obvious compound variants writers tend to produce: `wrongAnswer`, `bad_word`, `mistake_word`, `incorrect_word`, `correct_form`, `right_answer`, `corrected`, `corrected_form`, `proper`, `properForm`. Note also that `errorWord` / `error_word` are listed in `_ERROR_CORRECTION_INTENTIONAL_FIELDS` (line 532) yet are NOT in the optional item fields nor in the alias map, so they get flagged as a bare 'forbidden field' with no `expected:` hint — inconsistent with the rest of the policy. Add the common compounds and decide whether `errorWord`/`error_word` are aliases of `error` or genuinely forbidden.

Both `answer` and `correction` are accepted optional fields with identical declared purpose ('the corrected form' per `_ACTIVITY_ITEM_FIELD_PURPOSES`). The alias map routes `correct`, `correctAnswer`, `right`, `fix`, `fixed` → `correction`, but a writer using `correctAnswer` is more naturally pointed at `answer:` (semantic match) than `correction:`. This is cosmetic but the diagnostic message will tell the writer to use `correction:` when `answer:` would also satisfy the schema. Consider documenting that both are accepted in the diagnostic, or pick one canonical target and stop accepting the other.

`test_activity_schema_gate_precedes_vesum_verified` asserts the index of `activity_schema` in the `PYTHON_QG_GATE_ORDER` tuple, but actual gate execution order is driven by the explicit sequence of `record(...)` calls inside `run_python_qg`, not by iteration over the tuple. A future refactor that re-orders `record(...)` calls but leaves the constant alone will pass this test while breaking the intended early-fail ordering. Either drive execution from the tuple, or rewrite the test to inspect the keys of `gates` after a real `run_python_qg` invocation with a failing fixture.

On `activity_schema` failure the function early-returns, so the writer's correction loop only sees the activity schema violation and cannot fix word-count, plan-sections, or vesum issues in the same round. This forces an extra writer iteration (and another expensive build round) when the writer could have addressed multiple problems at once. Activity_schema is cheap and deterministic — once a writer fixes it, the next round will catch the rest — but if iteration cost matters here, consider letting the cheap structural gates all run and only short-circuiting before the expensive VESUM/RAG-style gates.
